title_en: Major Shafi'i Scholars and Their Contributions
content_en:
Through its ten volumes, Tabaqat ash-Shafi'iyyah al-Kubra documents the lives and contributions of hundreds of Shafi'i scholars from ash-Shafi'i's immediate students through the Mamluk period in which as-Subki himself lived. The biographies of major scholars in this tradition reveal the intellectual and institutional development of the Shafi'i school across several centuries.

Among ash-Shafi'i's immediate students, the biographies of al-Muzani, ar-Rabi' ibn Sulayman al-Muraddi, and others document how the school's positions were first transmitted and how the divergence between the Old Egyptian School (the positions ash-Shafi'i had held while in Iraq) and the New Egyptian School (the positions he revised and refined in Egypt) was handled by his students. This historical detail about the school's earliest transmission is available primarily through the tabaqat literature.

The biography of Abu Ishaq ash-Shirazi (393–476 AH), the author of Al-Muhadhdhab and one of the great Shafi'i jurists of the fifth century, illustrates the kind of extended treatment that major scholars receive. As-Subki documents ash-Shirazi's training, his teachers, his relationship with al-Juwayni, his role at the Baghdad Nizamiyyah, the works he produced, the students he trained, and the controversies he navigated. The biography brings to life the scholarly culture of the Nizamiyyah period and the interconnection of legal and theological scholarship.

The biography of al-Ghazali is also detailed and reflective, addressing the complexity of his relationship to the Shafi'i school — he was technically a Shafi'i jurist but his most famous works transcended legal school boundaries — and his intellectual and spiritual journey from kalam theology to Sufi synthesis.

The biographies of Egyptian Shafi'i scholars from the Ayyubid and Mamluk periods document the school's institutional presence in Cairo, where it was one of the dominant legal traditions and where major Shafi'i scholars taught at al-Azhar and other institutions. This historical documentation of the school's Egyptian development is one of the work's distinctive contributions.
